summary.intent: Review security vulnerabilities in the dorian-pepperberry branch of a Rust multi-channel bot (Moltis), then fix all identified issues related to session hijacking, unauthorized command approval, credential leakage, and missing agent ID resolution.
summary.outcome: All four security issues were fixed and committed: /attach scoped to same-account sessions, /approve and /deny restricted to allowlisted senders checked via channel registry, approval notifications truncated to 80 chars, and MS Teams updated to use resolve_model/resolve_agent_id.

repo: Moltis is a personal assistant bot (not a generic multi-user bot) — the DM allowlist is the canonical source of authorized users, expected to have ~1 member even when the bot is in group channels.
Channel authorization is enforced centrally in crates/gateway/src/channel_events.rs, not in individual channel plugins — channels should not embed authorization logic.
session_metadata.list_account_sessions(channel_type, account_id) exists as the scoped alternative to list() for filtering sessions by channel type and account.
channel_registry.account_config(account_id) returns Option<Box<dyn ChannelConfigView>> which exposes allowlist() — the canonical way to check if a sender is an authorized operator.
gating::is_allowed() returns true for empty allowlists (open policy), so privileged command auth must invert this: empty allowlist = no one explicitly authorized via channel.
Slack DM channel IDs start with 'D', group DMs with 'G', channels with 'C' — used as heuristic for DM detection.
On macOS, CUDA crate is excluded from clippy/test runs — this is expected per CLAUDE.md.
All channel handlers (Discord, Telegram, WhatsApp, Slack, Matrix, MS Teams) pass sender_id through to dispatch_command — the plumbing was already in place for authorization.
MS Teams was the only channel hardcoding agent_id: None and using config.model directly instead of resolve_model/resolve_agent_id, silently ignoring agent routing config.

code.finding: /attach command had no authorization check — listed all non-archived sessions via session_metadata.list() and allowed rebinding any session to the caller's chat.
/approve and /deny had no sender authorization — any group member could approve shell commands. Fixed by checking sender_id against channel allowlist via is_sender_on_allowlist() helper.
Approval notification broadcast the full command string (including potential secrets like Bearer tokens) to the channel. Fixed with truncate_command_preview() capping at 80 chars using Cow<'_, str>.
MS Teams hardcoded agent_id: None instead of config.resolve_agent_id(...), unlike all other channels. Fixed to use resolve_model and resolve_agent_id.
dispatch_command trait signature — a SenderAuth enum was added then removed after design iteration. Final design keeps the trait clean and enforces auth centrally in the gateway.

workflow: Replacing bool flags with self-documenting enums (SenderAuth) was explored but ultimately reverted — the better pattern was centralizing authorization logic in the gateway rather than pushing it to all channel implementations.
Design iterated through three approaches for approve/deny authorization: (1) is_dm bool, (2) SenderAuth enum, (3) allowlist check in gateway. User feedback drove the final design.
Running cargo test across all affected crates explicitly (channels, gateway, discord, telegram, whatsapp, slack, matrix, msteams) validated that 1762 tests passed after the full change set.
cargo fmt --all was run after edits and verified clean before committing — part of the project's standard workflow.

friction: SenderAuth enum approach required updating 11 files across all channel implementations, then was entirely reverted — significant churn from premature design commitment.
Iterating through three designs for the authorization model (is_dm bool → SenderAuth enum → allowlist check) required multiple rounds of widespread edits across the same files.
Telegram had many dispatch_command call sites requiring individual edits — the most tedious channel to update.
A subagent was used to revert SenderAuth changes across all channels, adding a layer of indirection that could have introduced inconsistencies.
truncate_command_preview test initially failed because a secret-key string appeared within the first 80 chars of the test input — required test case adjustment.
open_items: Channel command allowlist config: a config field to control which commands non-allowlisted users can run from group chats (e.g., /context, /compact). Intentionally deferred as a separate issue/PR due to schema, validation, template, and docs impact.

The is_sender_on_allowlist helper behavior when channel_registry is None (returns false/denies) should be documented — it's a silent deny that could confuse operators who haven't configured the registry.
Slack DM detection via channel_id.starts_with('D') is a heuristic — Slack's API does expose channel_type on some event types; a more robust check could be used if Slack event payloads are expanded.
Approval notification truncation at 80 chars is a hardcoded constant — could be made configurable per channel or per account in the future.
The /approve and /deny restriction to allowlisted senders means users with open DM policy (empty allowlist) can only approve via web UI — this should be documented in operator setup guides.
